query: fungal meningitis CSF characteristics, especially in patients with HIV

retrieved_docs.title: Cryptococcal Meningitis -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The initial prognosis depends on mortality predictors such as the following [2] , [4] , [17] , [18] : CSF opening pressure of more than 25 cm of water Low CSF white cells count Sensory impairment Delayed diagnosis Elevated CSF antigen titers Rate of infection clearance CSF yeast count more than 10 mm^3 (a common practice in Brazil) [19] Non-HIV-related patients and the prognostic factors in these patients, in addition to the already mentioned: Markers of a poor inflammatory response Absence of headache Underlying hematological malignancy Chronic renal or liver disease

retrieved_docs.title: HIV-1–Associated Opportunistic Infections -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Coccidioidomycosis: Isolation of the organism on culture is part of the diagnosis, and so is the visualization of the spherules on histopathologic evaluation of the affected tissue. Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) analysis for complement fixation IgG antibody is often used in the detection of coccidioidal meningitis. CSF cultures demonstrate positive results in less than 30% of patients with meningitis. Coccidioidal IgG and IgM serology are seldom positive in individuals with low CD4+ counts. Blood cultures are generally positive in diffuse pulmonary involvement. [21]

retrieved_docs.title: HIV-1–Associated Opportunistic Infections -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Histoplasma capsulatum: Disseminated histoplasmosis is diagnosable by detecting Histoplasma antigen in blood or urine. However, it is not sensitive to pulmonary infections. Peripheral blood smears may show the organisms engulfed by WBCs. The organism grows slowly and hence requires weeks for culture isolation; They have been cultured from bone marrow, blood, respiratory secretions in greater than 85% of AIDS patients. CSF cultures are rarely positive, and hence the diagnosis of meningitis is difficult. Detection of either anti-Histoplasma antibodies or Histoplasma antigen in the CSF is diagnostic and is positive in about 70% of cases. [22]

retrieved_docs.title: HIV Encephalitis -- Evaluation -- Lab Studies
retrieved_docs.content: CSF analysis by lumbar puncture plays an important role in evaluation. An increase in CSF protein, cell count, and the identification of HIV ribonucleic acid in CSF are all noted but are often non-specific findings related to HIV encephalopathy. In patients with HIV infection presenting with neurologic problems, CSF analysis helps rule out other opportunistic infections. The role of HIV culture in cerebral tissue samples of patients with HIV encephalitis has been extensively studied, with HIV cultures obtained from nerve, CSF, and brain tissues yielding positive results. However, this culture positivity has been noted in all the stages of HIV infection and does not correlate with the presence or absence of neurological symptoms or signs. Testing for CSF HIV viral levels and drug resistance is not routinely performed. An extensive metabolic panel needs to be undertaken in these patients to rule out conditions related to thyroid disease, folate, B12, syphilis, and hepatic disorders—which can present with a similar neurologic presentation.

retrieved_docs.title: Acquired Immune Deficiency Syndrome(Archived) -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: When evaluating neurologic complaints in the HIV or AIDS patient, providers should have a low threshold for computed tomography of the head and lumbar puncture with CSF analysis in HIV patients who may be immunocompromised, presenting with altered mental status, focal neurologic complaints, or concern for meningitis.  Cryptococcal meningitis may have a subtler presentation, and antigen testing should be considered for patients with altered mental status.

retrieved_docs.title: CNS Tuberculosis -- History and Physical -- Tuberculous Meningitis and HIV Infection
retrieved_docs.content: HIV coinfection does not affect CNS-TB's clinical manifestations, CSF picture, or prognosis. However, HIV-seropositive TBM may have a normal CSF, with radiographic clues holding greater importance in such cases. Cryptococcal meningitis is a close differential diagnosis and may sometimes occur concurrently. Intracerebral tuberculomas are more likely to occur in HIV patients than in the general population, often as multiloculated tubercular abscesses. CNS lymphoma or toxoplasma encephalitis closely mimics the condition. However, cisternal enhancement, basal ganglia infarction, and communicating hydrocephalus favor a CNS-TB diagnosis. [22]

retrieved_docs.title: HIV-1–Associated Opportunistic Infections -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Cryptococcus neoformans: Combination therapy of flucytosine and amphotericin B is the treatment of choice for cryptococcal meningitis. An alternative regimen of fluconazole and flucytosine are options, although it is inferior to amphoterin B. [30] Duration of combination therapy is 2 weeks, following which, after confirmation of a negative CSF culture on repeat lumbar puncture, they can be discontinued and started on fluconazole 400 mg daily for 8 weeks. Following this, the dose is reduced to 200 mg daily for 1 year. Secondary prophylaxis must be reinitiated when the CD4+ counts less than 100 cells/mm^3. [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Cryptococcal Meningitis -- Treatment / Management -- HIV-Related Disease
retrieved_docs.content: Maintenance therapy Fluconazole (200 mg/day) for 1 or more years (A1 Evidence)

retrieved_docs.title: Cryptococcal Meningitis -- Treatment / Management -- HIV-Related Disease
retrieved_docs.content: Consolidation therapy Fluconazole (400 mg/day) for 8 weeks (A1 Evidence)

retrieved_docs.title: Cryptococcal Meningitis -- Treatment / Management -- HIV-Related Disease
retrieved_docs.content: Induction therapy alternatives Amphotericin B deoxycholate + fluconazole (B1 Evidence) Fluconazole + flucytosine (B2 Evidence) Fluconazole (B2 Evidence) Itraconazole (C2 Evidence)

retrieved_docs.title: Meningitis (Nursing) -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Meningitis is diagnosed through cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) analysis, which includes white blood cell count, glucose, protein, culture, and in some cases, polymerase chain reaction (PCR). CSF is obtained via a lumbar puncture (LP), and the opening pressure can be measured. [10] [11] [12] Additional testing should be performed tailored on suspected etiology: Viral: Multiplex and specific PCRs Fungal: CSF fungal culture, India ink stain for Cryptococcus Mycobacterial: CSF Acid-fast bacilli smear and culture Syphilis: CSF VDRL Lyme disease: CSF burgdorferi antibody The CSF findings expected in bacterial, viral, and fungal meningitis are listed in the chart: Expected CSF findings in bacterial versus viral versus fungal meningitis.

retrieved_docs.title: HIV-1–Associated Progressive Polyradiculopathy -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Leptomeningeal carcinomatosis should be considered in a lumbosacral polyradiculopathy picture with no structural lesions on imaging and the presence of malignant cells in CSF. [56]

retrieved_docs.title: HIV-1–Associated Progressive Polyradiculopathy -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Lyme disease: Mimics GBS with similar CSF picture, diagnosis confirmed with positive serology for Lyme disease [58]

retrieved_docs.title: Cryptococcal Meningitis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Even though the infection is usually HIV-related, in many centers (especially in developed countries), most of the non-HIV-related cases include patients under immunosuppressive treatments or with organic failure syndromes, transplants, innate immunological problems, common variable immunodeficiency syndrome, and hematological disorders. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: CNS Tuberculosis -- Evaluation -- Cerebrospinal Fluid Examination and Culture
retrieved_docs.content: A pellicle or cobweb may form during gross CSF examination, which is representative but not pathognomonic of TBM. A xanthochromic CSF indicates unusually high protein content. CSF cytological analysis typically shows the total cell count between 100 and 500/mm3 in the majority. Less than 100 cells/mm3 are present in 15% of cases. Pleocytosis may be absent in people of advanced age or with miliary tuberculosis-related CNS-TB or HIV coinfection.

retrieved_docs.title: Meningitis -- Evaluation -- Computed Tomography (CT) of the Head before Lumbar Puncture
retrieved_docs.content: Blood work should include blood culture, serum electrolytes as the syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone secretion (SIADH) is not uncommon, serum glucose, renal and liver function, and testing for HIV.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 501
retrieved_docs.content: Meningitis, an inflammation of the protective membranes covering the brain and spinal cord, encompasses several specific forms, including bacterial meningitis (التهاب السحايا الجرثومي), which represents a distinct subtype caused by bacterial infection. Recognizing bacterial meningitis as a particular category within the broader condition of meningitis is essential for accurate diagnosis, targeted antimicrobial therapy, and effective patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2154
retrieved_docs.content: Abnormal and nonspecific findings in the cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) are clinically associated with headache, indicating that patients who exhibit such CSF abnormalities often present with this symptom. Likewise, lumbar puncture (LP) is also clinically associated with headache, reflecting the common occurrence of post‑procedural headache following the invasive sampling of CSF. In the broader diagnostic framework, “other screening for suspected conditions” includes abnormal and nonspecific CSF findings as a subcategory, linking this screening approach to the detection of CSF abnormalities that may underlie headache. Together, these relationships highlight the importance of recognizing headache as a key clinical manifestation linked both to intrinsic CSF pathology and to the lumbar puncture procedure itself, and they underscore the role of comprehensive screening strategies in identifying CSF abnormalities that may guide patient evaluation and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 1 -- Community 180
retrieved_docs.content: reflecting its central role in neurosurgical interventions. Screening for suspected neurological conditions frequently identifies abnormal CSF findings, reinforcing the importance of CSF analysis in diagnosing headache‑related disorders. Head injury (إصابة في الرأس) is associated with negative CSF findings and may lead to olfactory nerve disorder (اضطراب العصب الشمي), illustrating how traumatic brain injury can produce CSF changes and sensory deficits, guiding evaluation and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2204
retrieved_docs.content: Headache (صداع) is clinically associated with lumbar puncture (البزل القطني (LP)) and with abnormal and unspecified findings in the cerebrospinal fluid (اكتشافات غير طبيعية وغير محددة في السائل الدماغي النخاعي). In addition, the finding of increased intracranial pressure (تكثر الدبق الدماغي) may be a manifestation of disease that presents as headache. Abnormal and unspecified cerebrospinal‑fluid findings are categorized as a child of “other screening for suspected conditions,” indicating that such CSF abnormalities often arise from broader diagnostic evaluations. Together, these connections emphasize that headache can result from procedural complications (LP), reflect underlying CSF abnormalities, or signal increased intracranial pressure, highlighting the need for careful assessment of CSF studies and procedural history in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2894
retrieved_docs.content: Cerebral edema (تكثر الدبق الدماغي) may be a finding of disease that presents with headache (صداع), indicating that increased intracranial pressure or brain swelling can manifest as head pain. In addition, lumbar puncture (البزل القطني (LP)) is clinically associated with headache, reflecting the common post‑procedural symptom that patients may experience after cerebrospinal fluid sampling. Together, these connections underscore that headache can arise both from underlying intracranial pathology such as cerebral edema and from iatrogenic causes like lumbar puncture, highlighting the need for careful assessment of headache etiology in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2494
retrieved_docs.content: Triazole derivatives, systemic antimycotics constitute a class of antifungal agents that are administered systemically to treat invasive fungal infections. Both itraconazole and voriconazole are members of this triazole derivative class, sharing the characteristic triazole ring structure that confers broad-spectrum activity against a variety of pathogenic fungi. Their classification as systemic antimycotics underscores their use in managing serious fungal diseases where topical therapy is insufficient, highlighting the importance of these agents in comprehensive antifungal treatment strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 2 -- Community 1181
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIINFECTIVES FOR SYSTEMIC USE is a broad therapeutic class that includes both ANTIVIRALS FOR SYSTEMIC USE and antimycotics for systemic use. These subcategories represent systemic agents specifically targeting viral infections and fungal infections, respectively, and together they constitute the systemic anti‑infective armamentarium used in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 4 -- Community 2775
retrieved_docs.content: Allowed qualifier is recognized as an etiological factor for several neurological conditions. It is identified as a cause of الخرف من نوع الزهايمر (Alzheimer‑type dementia), of headache—including migraine—, of nervous system congenital anomalies, and of نزيف دموي تحت الجافية (subdural hemorrhage). Recognizing that the same allowed qualifier can underlie these diverse disorders aids clinicians in understanding their pathogenesis, guiding diagnostic evaluation, and informing targeted therapeutic strategies.
